| * [v](/wiki/Template:Chinese_characters_sidebar "Template:Chinese characters sidebar") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Chinese_characters_sidebar "Template talk:Chinese characters sidebar") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Chinese_characters_sidebar "Special:EditPage/Template:Chinese characters sidebar") |

The **second round of Chinese character simplification**[[b]](#cite_note-2) was an aborted [script reform](/wiki/Language_reform "Language reform") promulgated on 20 December 1977 by the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") (PRC). It was intended to replace the [first round of simplified characters](/wiki/Chinese_Character_Simplification_Scheme "Chinese Character Simplification Scheme") already in use. The complete proposal contained two lists: the first list consisted of 248 characters to be simplified, and the second list consisted of 605 characters to be evaluated and discussed. Of these characters, 21 from the first list and 40 from the second served as [components](/wiki/Chinese_character_components "Chinese character components"), which modified some 4,500 characters.

Following widespread confusion and opposition, the second round of simplification was officially rescinded on 24 June 1986 by the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China"). Since then, the PRC has used the first-round simplified characters as its official script. Rather than ruling out further simplification, however, the retraction declared that further reform of the Chinese characters should be done with caution. Today, some second-round simplified characters, while considered non-standard, continue to survive in informal usage.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Second_Chinese_Character_Simplification_Scheme_%28Draft%29_published_in_May_1977.pdf/page1-250px-Second_Chinese_Character_Simplification_Scheme_%28Draft%29_published_in_May_1977.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_Chinese_Character_Simplification_Scheme_(Draft)_published_in_May_1977.pdf)

Second Chinese Character Simplification Scheme (Draft), published in May 1977

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The traditional relationship between [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese") and [vernacular Chinese varieties](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") has been compared to that of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") with the [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") in the Renaissance era.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas1997248-3) The modern simplification movement grew out of efforts to make the written language more accessible, which culminated in the replacement of [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") with [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese") in the early 20th century.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199970–75-4) The fall of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") in 1911 and subsequent loss of prestige associated with classical writing helped facilitate this shift, but a series of further reforms aided by the efforts of reformers like [Qian Xuantong](/wiki/Qian_Xuantong "Qian Xuantong") were ultimately thwarted by conservative elements in the [new government](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912-1949) "Republic of China (1912-1949)") and the intellectual class.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999150–153-5)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohsenow200422-6)

Continuing the work of previous reformers, in 1956 the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") promulgated the Scheme of Simplified Chinese Characters, later referred to as the "First Round" or "First Scheme". The plan was adjusted slightly in the following years, eventually stabilizing in 1964 with a definitive list of character simplifications. These are the [simplified Chinese characters](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_character "Simplified Chinese character") that are used today in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").[[5]](#cite_note-Chen_154-155-7) [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") did not adopt the simplifications, and the characters used in those places are known as [traditional Chinese characters](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_character "Traditional Chinese character").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999162–163-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters_on_wall.jpg/250px-Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters_on_wall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters_on_wall.jpg)

The writing on this wall says "产量翻几番"/"產量翻幾番" ('production output will increase multiple times'), but uses non-standard characters. During the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), such sights were common as citizens were encouraged to innovate and participate in the character simplification process.[[7]](#cite_note-Ramsey_wall-9)

Also released in 1964 was a directive for further simplification in order to improve literacy, with the goal of eventually reducing the number of strokes in commonly used characters to ten or fewer. This was to take place gradually, with consideration for both "ease of production [writing] and ease of recognition [reading]." In 1975, a second round of simplifications, the Second Scheme, was submitted by the Script Reform Committee of China to the State Council for approval. Like the First Scheme, it contained two lists, where the first table (comprising 248 characters) was for immediate use, and the second table (comprising 605 characters) for evaluation and discussion.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999155–160-10) Of these characters, 21 from the first list and 40 from the second also served as [components](/wiki/Radical_(Chinese_character) "Radical (Chinese character)") of other characters, which caused the Second Scheme to modify some 4,500 characters.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199722–24-11) On 20 December 1977, major newspapers such as the *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")* and the *[Guangming Daily](/wiki/Guangming_Daily "Guangming Daily")* published the second-round simplifications along with editorials and articles endorsing the changes. Both newspapers began to use the characters from the first list the following day.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200762-12)

The Second Scheme was received extremely poorly, and as early as mid-1978, the Ministry of Education and the Central Propaganda Department were asking publishers of textbooks, newspapers, and other works to stop using the second-round simplifications. Second-round simplifications were taught inconsistently in the education system, and people used characters at various stages of official or unofficial simplification. Confusion and disagreement ensued.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200762–64-13)

The Second Scheme was officially retracted by the State Council on 24 June 1986. The State Council's retraction emphasized that Chinese character reform should henceforth proceed with caution, and that the forms of Chinese characters should be kept stable.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200751-14) Later that year, a final version of the 1964 list was published with minor changes, and no further changes have been made since.[[5]](#cite_note-Chen_154-155-7)

## Methods of simplification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Methods of simplification")]

|  |  |

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(April 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![Traditional characters (left) and their proposed simplifications (right)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/57/Erjian1.png)](/wiki/File:Erjian1.png "Traditional characters (left) and their proposed simplifications (right)")

Traditional characters (left) and their proposed simplifications (right)

The second round of simplification continued to use the methods used in the first round. For example:

In some characters, the phonetic component of the character was replaced with a simpler one, while the [radical](/wiki/Chinese_character_radicals "Chinese character radicals") was unchanged. For example:

* [菜](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%8F%9C "wikt:菜") → [𦬁](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A6%AC%81 "wikt:𦬁") (⿱艹才)
* [蔡](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%94%A1 "wikt:蔡") → 𦬁 (⿱艹才)
* 酒 → 氿
* 稳 → 𮂹 (⿰禾文)
* 儒 → 𰁡 (⿰亻入)
* 灌 → 浂
* 冀 → 丠
* 廖 → 𭙏 (⿸广了)
* 僚 → 𠆨 (⿰亻了)

In some characters, entire components were replaced by ones that are similar in shape:

* 幕 → 𫯜 (⿱大巾)
* 整 → 𰋞 (⿱大正)
* 迎 → 迊
* 答 → 荅
* 撤 → 𢪃 (⿰扌切)
* 阎 → 闫

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e6/Erjian2.png)](/wiki/File:Erjian2.png)

In some characters, components that are complicated are replaced with a simpler one not similar in shape but sometimes similar in sound:

* 鞋 → 𰆻 (⿰又圭)
* 短 → 𰦓 (⿰矢卜)
* 道 → 辺
* 嚷 → 𠮵 (⿰口上)

In some characters, the radical is simply dropped, leaving only the phonetic. This results in mergers between previously distinct characters:

* 稀 → 希
* 彩 → 采
* 帮 → 邦
* 蝌蚪 → 科斗
* 蚯蚓 → 丘引
* 豫 → 予

In some characters, entire components are dropped:

* 糖 → 𰪩 (⿰米广)
* 停 → 仃
* 餐 → 歺
* 雪 → 𫜹 (⿼コ一)
* 宣 → 㝉
* 囊 → 𰀉 (⿻一中)

Some characters are simply replaced by a similar-sounding one (a [rebus or phonetic loan](/wiki/Chinese_character_classification#Rebus_(phonetic_loan)_characters "Chinese character classification")). This also results in mergers between previously distinct characters:

* 萧 → 肖
* 蛋 → 旦
* 泰 → 太
* 雄 → 厷
* 鳜 → 桂
* 籍 → 笈
* 芭/粑/笆 → 巴
* 蝴/糊/猢 → 胡
* 衩/扠/杈/汊 → 叉

## Reasons for failure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reasons for failure")]

The Second Scheme broke with a millennia-long cycle of variant forms coming into unofficial use and eventually being accepted (90 percent of the changes made in the First Scheme existed in mass use, many for centuries[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999155–156-15)) in that it introduced new, unfamiliar character forms.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas1997223–224-16)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200767–68-17) The sheer number of characters it changed, the distinction between simplifications intended for immediate use and those for review not being maintained in practice, and its release in the shadow of the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") (1966–1978) have been cited among the chief reasons for its failure.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199722–24-11)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200766–69-18)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999160-19)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohsenow200429-20) As a result of the Cultural Revolution, trained experts were expelled and the Second Scheme was compiled by the committee and its staffers without outside consultation, which may also have been a factor.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999155–156-15)

The exact circumstances surrounding the creation and release of the Second Scheme remain in mystery due to the still-classified nature of many documents and the politically sensitive nature of the issue. However, the Second Scheme is known to have encompassed only about 100 characters before its expansion to over 850.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200754-21) A two-year delay from 1975 to 1977 was officially blamed on [Zhang Chunqiao](/wiki/Zhang_Chunqiao "Zhang Chunqiao"), a member of the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four"); however, there is little historical evidence to support this.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200758-22) Against the political backdrop of the Cultural Revolution, a special section known as the "748 Project" was formed with an emphasis on non-experts, under whose supervision the lists grew significantly. The bulk of the work is believed to have been performed by staffers without proper oversight.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohsenow200429-20)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200754–62-23)

The Second Scheme's subsequent rejection by the public has been cited as a case study in a failed attempt to artificially control the direction of a language's evolution.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHodgeLouie199863–64-24) It was not embraced by the linguistic community in China upon its release;[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200763-25) despite heavy promotion in official publications, Rohsenow observes that "in the case of some of the character forms constructed by the staff members themselves" the public at large found proposed changes "laughable".[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohsenow200428–29-26)

Political issues aside, Chen Ping objects to the notion that all characters should be reduced to ten or fewer strokes. He argues that a technical shortcoming of the Second Scheme was that the characters it reformed occur less often in writing than those of the First Scheme. As such it provided less benefit to writers while putting an unnecessary burden on readers in making the characters more difficult to distinguish.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen1999160–162-27) Citing several studies, Hannas similarly argues against the lack of differentiation and utility: "it was meaningless to lower the stroke count for its own sake." Thus, he believes simplification and reduction of the number of characters[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas1997215-28) both amount to a [zero-sum game](/wiki/Zero-sum_game "Zero-sum game")—simplification in one area of use causing complication in another—and concludes that "the 'complex' characters in [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") and Chinese, with their greater redundancy and internal consistency, may have been the better bargain."[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas1997226–229-29)

## Effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Effects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/%E4%BB%83%E8%BD%A6%E5%BE%80%E5%8F%B3.jpg/250px-%E4%BB%83%E8%BD%A6%E5%BE%80%E5%8F%B3.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%BB%83%E8%BD%A6%E5%BE%80%E5%8F%B3.jpg)

A sign reading "仃车往右" ('parking lot to the right'), which, instead of using [停](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%81%9C "wikt:停"), uses [仃](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%83 "wikt:仃"), one of the most successfully survived second-round characters now.

While the stated goal of further language reform was not changed, the 1986 conference which retracted the Second Scheme emphasized that future reforms should proceed with caution.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf200764-30) It also "explicitly precluded any possibility of developing [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin") as an independent writing system (*wénzì*)."[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohsenow200430-31) The focus of [language planning](/wiki/Language_planning "Language planning") policy in China following the conference shifted from simplification and reform to standardization and regulation of existing characters,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERohsenow200432-32) and the topic of further simplification has since been described as "untouchable" in the field.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhaoBaldauf2007299-300-33) However, the possibility of future changes remains,[[32]](#cite_note-34) and the difficulties the Chinese writing system presents for [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") have renewed the [Romanization](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") debate.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199725-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%82%E6%9D%A8%E6%B5%A6%E5%8C%BA%E5%9B%BD%E5%AE%9A%E8%B7%AF504%E5%BC%84%EF%BC%88%E9%9D%A0%E8%BF%91%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E8%B7%AF%EF%BC%89-%E4%B8%80%E4%BD%B3%E6%AD%BA%E5%8E%85.JPG/250px-%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%82%E6%9D%A8%E6%B5%A6%E5%8C%BA%E5%9B%BD%E5%AE%9A%E8%B7%AF504%E5%BC%84%EF%BC%88%E9%9D%A0%E8%BF%91%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E8%B7%AF%EF%BC%89-%E4%B8%80%E4%BD%B3%E6%AD%BA%E5%8E%85.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%82%E6%9D%A8%E6%B5%A6%E5%8C%BA%E5%9B%BD%E5%AE%9A%E8%B7%AF504%E5%BC%84%EF%BC%88%E9%9D%A0%E8%BF%91%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%A6%E8%B7%AF%EF%BC%89-%E4%B8%80%E4%BD%B3%E6%AD%BA%E5%8E%85.JPG)

A playful name for a restaurant in Shanghai that says "一佳歺厅" homophonous to "一家餐厅" (a restaurant).

Today, second round characters are officially regarded as incorrect. However, some have survived in informal contexts; this is because some people who were in school between 1977 and 1986 received their education in second-round characters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In three cases, the second round split one family name into two. The first round of simplification had already changed the common surnames [蕭](/wiki/Xiao_(surname) "Xiao (surname)") (*Xiāo*; [30th most common in 1982](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames")) and [閻](/wiki/Yan_(surname) "Yan (surname)") (*Yán*; 50th) into [萧](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%90%A7 "wikt:萧") and [阎](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%98%8E "wikt:阎"). The second round adjusted these further and combined them with other characters previously much less common as surnames: [肖](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%82%96 "wikt:肖") and [闫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%97%AB "wikt:闫"). Similarly, though originally intended to simplify [副](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%89%AF "wikt:副"), [傅](/wiki/Fu_(surname) "Fu (surname)") (*Fù*; 36th) was changed to [付](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%98 "wikt:付") as well.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Technical information

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Technical information")]

Most systems of [Chinese character encoding](/wiki/Chinese_character_encoding "Chinese character encoding"), including [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") and [GB 18030](/wiki/GB_18030 "GB 18030"), provide full support for the first list of second-round characters,[[35]](#cite_note-37) and only partial support for the second list, with many such characters unencoded or yet to be standardized. [Mojikyo](/wiki/Mojikyo "Mojikyo") supports the characters on the first list.

## Example text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_round_of_simplified_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Example text")]

From Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") in Second Round Simplified Chinese:

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b5/SecSimChinesetexttest.jpg)](/wiki/File:SecSimChinesetexttest.jpg)

:   **First-round Simplified Chinese script**: 人人生来自由，在尊严和权利上一律平等。他们有理性和良心，请以手足关系的精神相对待。
:   **Traditional Chinese script**: 人人生來自由，在尊嚴和權利上一律平等。他們有理性和良心，請以手足關係的精神相對待。
